description: This scenario tracks public opinion during the U.S. government's landmark antitrust lawsuit against Microsoft (1998-2001), which alleged that the company illegally bundled Internet Explorer with Windows to maintain a monopoly. The litigation eventually led to a 2001 settlement that required Microsoft to share application programming interfaces with third-party companies.

key_events.description: DOJ files initial antitrust lawsuit against Microsoft regarding Internet Explorer bundling.
Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson issues findings of fact stating Microsoft holds monopoly power.
Judge Jackson issues conclusions of law, declaring Microsoft violated the Sherman Antitrust Act.
Appeals court overturns the breakup order but upholds the finding that Microsoft is a monopolist.
Microsoft and the Department of Justice reach a final settlement agreement.

calibration_notes: Useful for calibrating how public support shifts during prolonged high-profile legal battles between government regulators and tech giants, specifically regarding market dominance.
